item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as, per the Global Egyptian Museum's copyright information, all images on their site are copyrighhted by their respective collections. Thus, the image has been removed.

EDIT: The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Flickr
License: Public Domain
Title: 2017
Author: Carlo Raso
Release year: 2017

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone with a huge interest and knowledge of egyptian history, this was very well done. How are we gonna tell him his pyramid is being used as a waste dump?

item.title: Re: Author Overview
item.content:encoded: Good job dude, who was it that came up the movement in the sarcophagus? Brilliant idea, I think there could be more depth from a test experiment of moving a unconscious test subject to it, and then waking them up just for gigs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

There are thematic similarities between this and SCP-1962, both concern Egyptian pharaohs who have tried to use magic to create power for themselves, and failed horribly with grave consequences. Due to this, I propose that the Foundation would store information related to these objects in the same location– Reliquary Site-62 from SCP-1962.

Editing this:

The ruins found within SCP-1644 contain many artifacts that do not exhibit anomalous properties. These artifacts are currently being stored at Site 57's historical artifact department.

To this:

The ruins found within SCP-1644 contain many artifacts that do not exhibit anomalous properties. These artifacts are currently being stored at Reliquary Site-62’s historical artifact department.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, baka joke aside, I don't know why I downvoted this and have since remedied that mistake. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since I read this without opening the collapsibles first and then went back to see what was there, I could see the twist coming from a mile away. However, the world-building done is really good, so the relative cliche there really doesn't affect the good effect of the overall piece.

item.title: Re: Ammit
item.content:encoded: Even if not 682 explicitly, I was hoping this would build upon good ol' SCP-557 or touch upon SCP-911. Neither of these are obligatory, but they would have made sense to have cross-links.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well of course Ba-Ka is alive and thrashing about in his sarcophagus. The Foundation keeps using his name and they have it recorded. To remember the name of the dead is to make them live again in Egyptian lore (if I remember correctly).

item.title: Re: Author Overview
item.content:encoded: Slight necro, but that was totally intentional on the Egyptians' part. If I recall correctly, a person can't get into the afterlife if records/proof, including their body and any documents, weren't preserved according to ancient Egyptian culture. Cremation and censorship like that were used as a way to condemn people to purgatory.

item.title: Ammit
item.content:encoded: The thing that stood out most for me was a line from the Fifth Volume: "Ammit is unleashed upon this world…". My first thought was that this was a very oblique reference to SCP-682. If the researches also made this connection, then the possibility of a tome that could grant immortality and conjure Keter-level abominations would be enough to justify the high levels of secrecy being applied to the project. Even without a 682 connection, anything that can unleash "unholy plagues of the gods" is still a cause for concern, and I see the justification for the security means being used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No real hook, and a tad bit long. However, the creativeness of SCP-1644 deserves a good rating on it's own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would have been better if it had explored the anger of the gods more instead of focusing on the immortality. You have a very good start and I love the recovered documents, but the ending was a flop. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is an immortal dude working for the Foundation, IIRC.
Foundation also routinely performs Eldritch Rituals of Unspeakable Evil to keep even less speakable evil at bay.

I, too, am among the people who just don't get what's so special about this immortal guy and his little amateurisly botched ritual.

It would have worked better if it was just "creepy obelisk what spooks", or if the hook was something that happens to people who are forced to approach.

As it is now, no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Japanese were all like "Man, that country is 2tsuntsun4me"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Suffice to say, Japanese-Egyptian relations under Ba-Ka were awkward.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "I hope Thoth-sempaii notices me today. T-that jerk."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "N-not that I like you or something, y-you Ba-Ka!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
so, he became immortal and nonbelievers cannot go near him.
That would be a pretty embarrassing containment breach.

"alright, we got it cornered. get the ropefreak!"
"okay! I'll go firs- no, no, NO GET ME AWAY FROM IT!" (freaks out)
"what the hell? he's not doing anything dangerous. He just stepped forwa- SWEET MOTHER OF GOD IM OUT!"

and then they all run away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually found this interesting all the through. The ending sealed the deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't say I found this terribly interesting. The main description is fairly dull, and the things Anaxagoras points out didn't really convince me that I needed to read pages and pages about whatever baka made this thing to uncover the full story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Threats of violence, up to, and including death as a punishment for refusing to enter SCP-1644's active area have failed, as individuals will continue to refuse to enter SCP-1644, even when such threats have been carried out.

This is unintentionally hilarious. "If you don't go into the area, I will kill you." "No! I… I can't." *BANG* "Still won't go in?" … "All right, mark that down, he's continuing to refuse."

This doesn't really improve it too much either. Now we've got some mythical book that allegedly contains an immortality ritual. I still don't see how this warrants the AMNESTICS-ARE-CANDY containment, and the general over-the-topness of this. Cripes, we've got so many immortality methods already, and in real life, there's so many legends of rulers who sought immortality. What makes this special?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it. Especially the last addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clarified it a bit. Does that make it better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing that bugs me: according to the way it's written, the Magic Line only makes you not want to go closer. I'd think threats of death would easily make you do something you didn't want to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep; I made some edits. Look down below, I made a comment about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies. I don't think of myself as particularly negative, but there is one rather big problem with this article. And I'm not the only person to have this issue with the article. If you got the other one fixed up, you can do this one too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Made some edits, based on the criticisms made by Anaxagoras and Myriad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Achieving immortality and yet being permanently contained in a cell? Not the first time I've heard of it, but the articles did it for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but outside of the Foundation, who's actually going to know that he succeeded? Even if some tablet says that the ritual was successful, no one's likely to believe it. And heck even the records that the foundation found suggests that he died a horrible death rather than succeeded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Currently on my phone, but I've got an idea. Always an uphill battle to wrangle an upvote out of you, huh? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The difference is, Ba-Ka succeeded. Not in the way that he might have liked, but he succeeded nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I think trying to erase information about it is actually the worse way to go. After all, in terms of immortality there's a whole crapton of people in history, myth, and legend who have tried to gain it. Ba-Ka would just be be one more on the pile. That said, if someone twigs to the fact that men in dark suits are very seriously trying to erase everything about this guy then… hey, maybe there actually might _be_ something to that one guy's quest for immortality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But the Foundation already has a bunch of methods of gaining immortality. I don't see how one more, which is way more nebulous and unknown than the bunch we already know about is such a serious issue that the Foundation has to lock down everything, Class A everyone, and try to destroy public knowledge. There's all kinds of legends about attempts to gain immortality. What's one more in the pile?

Basically, until there's a reason for the SUPER-SECRETness, which seems to be the main hook, to be so, I'm downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is, if people know that Ba-Ka attempted to gain immortality, they're going to start digging. The Foundation knows little to nothing about the mage. If they start digging, they could find some record or legend that refers to how Ba-Ka attempted to gain immortality, and that would be very, very, bad. Therefore, the safest route to go is to simply erase Ba-Ka from memory. If no one knows about the immortality-crazed pharaoh, then no one's going to go looking for how he tried to gain immortality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not seeing why the information that's a level 4 clearance needs that clearance. It doesn't seem like information that would tempt less trustworthy people to do something, or be dangerous knowledge to have, and it isn't an infohazard or memetic hazard. And I'm also not seeing why public knowledge of Ba-Ka is to be minimized, unless legends about Ba-Ka either hint as to where to find the SCP or hint as to how to duplicate what Ba-Ka did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god, why do I do this every time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
X's, man!

Thank you. Not a bad skip. A little narrative-driven.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take yer upvote. I was impressed

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Voct, CryogenChaos, Djoric, TwistedGears, Iceforge, Wogglebug, and many, many other chat users who read through this and helped craft this skip into what it is now.

The inspiration for this came from the idea of damnatio memoriae: I was reading about the Turin King List (aka a list of Egyptian pharaohs), and a few entries were wiped out, or too damaged to read. My imagination went "Hey, what if that was intentional," and this happened.

EDIT: Oh, and the image is from the Global Egyptian Museum.
